feed.title: Giorgio Baldelli’s notes
feed.subtitle: Custom Theme line in _config.yml. It will appear in your document head meta (for Google search results) and in your feed.xml site description.

entry.title: Why I Like Bicycles
entry.content:
This is my first note and also a love declaration for bicycles, I guess.

I remember the first time I rode a bicycle without training wheels as if it was yesterday. The moment is imprinted in my mind and I can exactly see the yellow, red and blue tones of my first BMX in front of me. Although I grew up in Rome, which is not exactly a bike-friendly city, biycles have played important part in my life and they still do today.

When I was a student in the Netherlands, living on a tight budget, my bike allowed me to move freely around the city. Riding a bike was a form of entertainment, a way to stay fit and a necessity. My heart was crushed when my city bike got stolen. I quickly moved on, however, and bought a second-hand one and lived frugually for a while.

In Berlin, where I moved to make my first professional steps after graduating, bicyles continued playing an important role. Despite employers offering public transportation benefits, I’ve always preferred moving around the city by biycle: be it scorching hot, rainy, snowy or cold, I prefer biycles than the suffocating feeling of a crowded subway compartment.

Call me crazy if you’d like, but I think that there is nothing that comes as close to the feeling of freedom as riding a bicyle. I look forward to many more years of bike riding and fun tours with friends or solo.

800 km - Berlin to Bologna

600 km - Coyahique to O’Higgins

800 km - Berlin to Gothenburg
entry.summary: This is my first note and also a love declaration for bicycles, I guess. I remember the first time I rode a bicycle without training wheels as if it was yesterday. The moment is imprinted in my mind and I can exactly see the yellow, red and blue tones of my first BMX in front of me. Although I grew up in Rome, which is not exactly a bike-friendly city, biycles have played important part in my life and they still do today. When I was a student in the Netherlands, living on a tight budget, my bike allowed me to move freely around the city. Riding a bike was a form of entertainment, a way to stay fit and a necessity. My heart was crushed when my city bike got stolen. I quickly moved on, however, and bought a second-hand one and lived frugually for a while. In Berlin, where I moved to make my first professional steps after graduating, bicyles continued playing an important role. Despite employers offering public transportation benefits, I’ve always preferred moving around the city by biycle: be it scorching hot, rainy, snowy or cold, I prefer biycles than the suffocating feeling of a crowded subway compartment. Call me crazy if you’d like, but I think that there is nothing that comes as close to the feeling of freedom as riding a bicyle. I look forward to many more years of bike riding and fun tours with friends or solo. 800 km - Berlin to Bologna 600 km - Coyahique to O’Higgins 800 km - Berlin to Gothenburg

entry.title: Pros & Cons of Static Website Generators

link.title: Pros & Cons of Static Website Generators

entry.content:
I’ve built this website using a static site generator. Setting it up was a fast and hassle-free process.

What’s a static site generator?

A static site generator parses markdown files and generates static html output ready to be render by any browser. Tools like Jekyll make this process simple and automatic.

The three benefits of using a static site generator

  • It’s cheap to host a static site.
  • Maintenance of a static site is easy.
  • You’re not bound to a specific tech stack.

Some design work is required to prepare templates in which the content will be injected of course. However, I find the process quite simple and satisfying. Jekyll uses the template rendering engine Liquid to simplify this work. The syntax is very similar to Jinja. Both of these engines allow you to easily add custom rendering logic to your templates and do cool things like these:

<div class="post-content">
    {%- if site.show_excerpts -%}
        {{ post.excerpt | strip_html | truncate: 300}}
    {%- endif -%}
</div>

The code above generates the excerpts on the homepage. It checks whether the the variable site.show_excerpts == true. If the value is true, it then strips away html tags, truncates the excert to a maximum of 300 characters and lastly displays the content. In short Liquid allows you to supercharge your rendering logic.

Additionals comments on the benefits of static sites

  • I’ve mentioned this before: it’s incredibly cheap to host this website. The only cost that I’m incurring is the custom domain name. All files are stored in this public repository.
  • I can easily clone the whole repository and download all content stored in the posts directory if I decide to migrate one day. Markdown is a popular markup languague. This makes it easy to convert files to other standards if necessary.
  • Keep it simple, stupid! There is no server-side code running to generate any of the content. This drastically reduces maintenance time. I can just focus on the actual content.

The second point that I’ve made is at the same time the main disadvantage. Given that we’re limited to static content, server-side interactions with database are not possible. For example, if wanted to allow visitors of this website to add comments to each post or register a profile, I’d have to integrate third party tools.

Despite this limitation, static website are a great solution if your website serves as a portfolio, blog or digital notebook. I’m a fan of Jekyll and I love to be able to focus on creating content rather than maintaing this website.
entry.summary: I’ve built this website using a static site generator. Setting it up was a fast and hassle-free process. What’s a static site generator? A static site generator parses markdown files and generates static html output ready to be render by any browser. Tools like Jekyll make this process simple and automatic. The three benefits of using a static site generator It’s cheap to host a static site. Maintenance of a static site is easy. You’re not bound to a specific tech stack. Some design work is required to prepare templates in which the content will be injected of course. However, I find the process quite simple and satisfying. Jekyll uses the template rendering engine Liquid to simplify this work. The syntax is very similar to Jinja. Both of these engines allow you to easily add custom rendering logic to your templates and do cool things like these: <div class="post-content"> {%- if site.show_excerpts -%} {{ post.excerpt | strip_html | truncate: 300}} {%- endif -%} </div> The code above generates the excerpts on the homepage. It checks whether the the variable site.show_excerpts == true. If the value is true, it then strips away html tags, truncates the excert to a maximum of 300 characters and lastly displays the content. In short Liquid allows you to supercharge your rendering logic. Additionals comments on the benefits of static sites I’ve mentioned this before: it’s incredibly cheap to host this website. The only cost that I’m incurring is the custom domain name. All files are stored in this public repository. I can easily clone the whole repository and download all content stored in the posts directory if I decide to migrate one day. Markdown is a popular markup languague. This makes it easy to convert files to other standards if necessary. Keep it simple, stupid! There is no server-side code running to generate any of the content. This drastically reduces maintenance time. I can just focus on the actual content. The second point that I’ve made is at the same time the main disadvantage. Given that we’re limited to static content, server-side interactions with database are not possible. For example, if wanted to allow visitors of this website to add comments to each post or register a profile, I’d have to integrate third party tools. Despite this limitation, static website are a great solution if your website serves as a portfolio, blog or digital notebook. I’m a fan of Jekyll and I love to be able to focus on creating content rather than maintaing this website.

entry.title: Avoiding Survivorship Bias in Time Series Analysis

link.title: Avoiding Survivorship Bias in Time Series Analysis

entry.content:
I’ve had this experience many times: during a presentation charts are presented with a temporal dimension on the x-axis to portray the evolution of certain charactersitic over time. However, when looking deeper and looking at how the data and the final analysis are generated, survivorship bias is unfortunately not an uncommon occurence.

What’s survivorship bias?

Survivorship bias occurs when you focus purely on data observations that survive specific selection criteria and ignore observations that do not survive. If it all sounds too abstract so, let me give you an example from the business world:

Let’s say you have a company with a team of Customer Success Managers. Their role is to help customers be succesful and provide support. Each Customer Success Managers uses the internal CRM platform to keep track of the Accounts & Contacts that they own.

At the end of each quarter, as a Team Lead or Manager, you’re interested in figuring out which Customer Success initative was sucesful by looking at data. You build a report that looks at all Accounts currently owned by Customer Success Managers and try to draw conclusions from it.

The risk here is that you may ignore the fact that Customer Success Managers may have dropped some Accounts during the quarter by simply unticking a checkbox in the CRMs system. If you ignore this possibility, as a manager, you will simply focus on Account that are currently under Active Coverage and your analyis will ignore those that were dropped. You’ll most likely gain a very rosy picture of how your strategy has been doing.

To avoid the trap of survivorship bias, it’s essential to have timeseries data. In this speicific example, it would help to have timestamps that log when an Account/Contact is picked up or dropped by a Customer Success Manager. By incorporating this data, we’ll be able to include both Accounts that have survived and died helping us gain a more realistic understanding.

In many projects that I’ve worked on, the first step towards solving survivorship bias is enabling field tracking history in the specific CRM platform that is being used by the company. This will ensure records being created when a specific value in a specific field is changed. Example:

Account_Id Created_date Old_value New_value
a12389653 2020-01-05 Claudia  
a12389653 2020-06-03 Mark Claudia
a12389653 2020-01-01   Mark

With this data, we’re able to track exacty when a specifc Account/Contact was covered and by which Customer Success Manager. By incorporating these time ranges, we will able to produce historical analyses based on more realistic assumptions and minimze the risk of survivorship bias.
entry.summary: I’ve had this experience many times: during a presentation charts are presented with a temporal dimension on the x-axis to portray the evolution of certain charactersitic over time. However, when looking deeper and looking at how the data and the final analysis are generated, survivorship bias is unfortunately not an uncommon occurence.
